metadata.full_json:
{"id": "img_174", "source_md": "On-road emissions of fine particles and associated.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\On-road emissions of fine particles and associated\figure_7.jpg", "title": "Fig. 7. Percentages of different categories in  PM_{2.5}  emitted from vehicles.", "references": ["The components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  could be divided into five categories: OC, EC, water-soluble ions, inorganic elements and other components. Each percentages of these five categories in the particulate matter were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 7. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in gasoline vehicles were  $53.93\%$ $13.07\%$ $13.12\%$  and  $0.30\%$  respectively. The proportions of OC, EC, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements in diesel vehicles were  $51.15\%$ $25.17\%$ $14.53\%$  and  $0.44\%$  respectively. Thus, the largest difference in the proportion of various components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline and diesel vehicles was EC.", "Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.", "As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.", "The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the", "The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.", "The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. content.title: Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.
content.summary: The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.
content.insight: Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_180", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  8hO_3  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  PM_{2.5}  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  PM_{2.5}  and  O_3  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Despite the substantial progress that has been made in addressing acid rain and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  issues (Fig. 6(a)), an unwanted increase in ground-level  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution is still found in many regions across China, posing the more complex challenge of simultaneous continuous mitigation of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution with an emerging  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issue. As  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution has been steadily increasing in most parts of eastern China [47-50],  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  issues have recently gained more attention among both the public and academia in China. As illustrated in Fig. 6(b), the average 90th percentiles in daily maximum  $8\\mathrm{h}$ $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations increased by  $20.1\\%$  and  $24.5\\%$  in 74 key cities and BTH, respectively, from 2013 to 2017. Furthermore, densely populated regions of eastern China were exposed to high  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution, increasing the impacts of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  on human health. The results of the GBD project [51] estimated that respiratory mortality attributable to long-term  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  exposure in adults is between 274000 and 316000 in China, equal to approximately  $25\\% -29\\%$  of the deaths attributable to  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  exposure [16]. It is necessary to integrate  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  controls to ensure a pollution-free and healthy living environment in the development of ecological civilization in China.", "The biggest challenge of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  pollution control is rooted in the complexity of atmospheric photochemistry formation, which depends on  $\\mathrm{O_3 - NO_x - VOC}$  sensitivity diagnosis and meteorological conditions [52,53]. A control strategy designed to reduce emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  or VOCs for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  control may result in an unexpected disadvantage for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  reduction. Strong VOC-limiting systems are found in most urban areas of China [54,55], and  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  in urban areas can be enhanced if few or no VOCs are controlled simultaneously with  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  [56]. For example, during the Action Plan from 2013 to 2017, anthropogenic emissions of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  were reduced by  $21\\%$  but VOCs increased by  $2\\%$  nationwide [57]. Such combinational changes in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -VOC emissions might be beneficial for reducing  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  but not for  $\\mathrm{O_3}$ , as shown by the decreased  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and increased  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations during the same period (Fig. 6). It is notable that the increase in  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentration during the Action Plan period is also attributable to variation in meteorological conditions—specifically, changing temperature and short-wave radiation. Therefore, full consideration of meteorology and optimal mitigation of precursor emissions are critical in designing an effective  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  control strategy."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Line Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5", "O3", "BTH region", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "90th percentiles"], "concise_summary": "The figure shows trends in PM2.5 and O3 concentrations between 2013 and 2017 in BTH and 74 key cities, highlighting a decrease in PM2.5 and an increase in O3 levels.", "inferred_insight": "Despite reductions in PM2.5, O3 pollution increased, posing challenges in simultaneous mitigation and highlighting the need for integrated pollution control strategies in China."}

content.title: Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\%  CIs around the central estimates.
content.summary: The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.
content.insight: This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality.

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_192", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of a bottom-up emiss\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Comparisons of emission uncertainties for different species in China from different studies, expressed as  95\\%  CIs around the central estimates.", "references": ["for 2003 instead of 2005. Moreover, open burning of biomass was not included in Ohara et al. (2007). Regarding  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , Ohara et al. (2007) estimated a total emission of  $36.6\\mathrm{Mt}$  for China in 2003, much higher than all other estimates for 2005 and later. This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. (2007) relied more upon. Figure 5 compares the resulting emission inventory uncertainties with those of other studies, none of which included Monte Carlo simulation. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\\%$  CIs of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\\% \\sim 130\\%$ ,  $-68\\% \\sim 208\\%$ , and  $-72\\% \\sim 258\\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The main reasons include: (1) the source categories in this study are more detailed, and random errors are thus significantly reduced due to the \"compensation-of-error\" mechanism (the same statistical mechanism described for the power sector comparison in Sect. 4.4); and (2) emission factors are", "Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting", "Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\\% \\sim 13\\%$  for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\\% \\sim 495\\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\\%$  to  $7.1\\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\\% \\sim 147\\%$  and  $-44\\% \\sim 104\\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on", "In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.", "Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\\% \\sim 13\\%$ ,  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ ,  $-11\\% \\sim 38\\%$ ,  $-14\\% \\sim 45\\%$ ,  $-17\\% \\sim 54\\%$ ,  $-25\\% \\sim 136\\%$ , and  $-40\\% \\sim 121\\%$  for total emissions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).", "the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\\sim 216\\%$  to  $-31\\%\\sim 311\\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).", "The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.", "Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates of this study.", "Although inverse studies that evaluate emission inventories using observations and CTMs have indicated that Chinese  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions may be underestimated by around  $50\\%$  (Wang et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2006), the uncertainty estimated in this study is relatively small, ranging only  $-13\\% \\sim 37\\%$ . The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. These would require region-specific uncertainty assumptions for emission factors and activity levels, and seasonal distribution parameters."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Compare Experimental Performance", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Bar Chart"]}, "key_entities": ["emission uncertainties", "SO2", "NOx", "PM10", "PM2.5", "BC", "OC", "China", "Monte Carlo simulation"], "concise_summary": "The figure compares emission uncertainties for various species in China across different studies, showing 95% confidence intervals.", "inferred_insight": "This figure illustrates the reduced uncertainties in emission estimates for PM species and carbonaceous compounds due to improved categorizations and data quality."}

metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_263", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission\\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. The probability bands of particle removal efficiencies of wet scrubbers for cement production (a-c) and of fabric filters for sintering (d-f), through bootstrap simulation with individual plant data.", "supplement": "(a)  PM_{2.5}  (WET for CEM);(b)  PM_{2.5 - 10}  (WET for CEM);(c)  PM_{>10}  (WET for CEM);(d)  PM_{2.5}  (FF for sintering);(e)  PM_{2.5 - 10}  (FF for sintering);(f)  PM_{>10}  (FF for sintering)", "references": ["The removal efficiencies of dust collector by particle size are crucial for estimating the size-fractioned PM emissions of single plant. Nevertheless, there was little information on removal rates of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  or  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  but TSP for individual plants in current databases, and limited field measurements across the country had to be relied on (Zhao et al., 2010). In this study, the investigated removal rates for TSP by plant and the PM size fractions by dust collector type from previous work were incorporated to calculate the removal rates for size-fractioned PM by plant. For data samples that passed K-S test, bootstrap simulations were applied to quantify the mean values and  $95\\%$  CIs of the removal rates by dust collector type and particle size. Fig. 2 provides bootstrap simulation examples for particle removal efficiencies of wet scrubber for cement production and those of fabric filter for sintering. For those that failed to pass K-S test, normal and uniform distributions were subjectively applied for the parameters with sample size  $\\geq 10$  and  $< 10$ , respectively. The  $95\\%$  CIs were calculated based on the relative ranges provided in our previous work by dust collector type (Zhao et al., 2010). metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_266", "source_md": "Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Quantifying the uncertainties of China's emission\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Comparisons of Jiangsu's annual emissions from different studies. The range line represents the  95%  CIs around the central estimates of the provincial inventory by this work.", "references": ["Fig. 5 compares the central estimates of Jiangsu's emissions (at provincial level) by this work with those of other studies. As limited information was available for the same year as this work (2012), the results for 2010 and 2014 were included for comparisons as well. Those inventories were developed either at national scale (MEIC; Xia et al., 2016) or regional scale (Fu et al., 2013; Shanghai Research Academy of Environmental Science, SRAES). It can be seen that most estimates are within the  $95\\%$  CIs calculated in the current study for gaseous pollutants, BC and OC. Inter-annual variability could partly explain the lower estimates by SRAES and Fu et al. (2013). Different data sources (in particular the penetrations and removal rates of emission control technologies) in multiple-scale inventories also contributed to the discrepancies in emission estimates. For  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , emissions from other studies were much smaller than this work at provincial level, even out of the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates. This large discrepancy came mainly from the difference in the removal rates of dust collectors used in various inventories. While all other studies applied the national/regional averages of dust removal rates based on limited test samples to calculate the emissions, the plant-specific data obtained from on-site investigations generally implied much lower removal rates for the province, leading to larger emission estimates in the provincial inventory. The comparison revealed the importance of developing and applying local emission factors on establishment of high-resolution emission inventory whenever possible.", "Fig. 4. The distributions of  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (a) and  $\\mathrm{NO_X}$  (b) emissions from power plants in Jiangsu in 2012, using Monte-Carlo simulation. The red and black bars represent the results from the national and provincial inventories, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)  \nFig. 5. Comparisons of Jiangsu's annual emissions from different studies. The range line represents the  $95\\%$  CIs around the central estimates of the provincial inventory by this work.", "Following our previous work (Zhao et al., 2011), Monte Carlo simulation was applied to quantify the uncertainties of national inventory by source category for the whole country and Jiangsu province. In the simulation framework, uncertainty of each parameter was characterized by probability distribution function (PDF), and the framework randomly generated values for uncertain variables over and over to simulate a model. A simulation calculated numerous scenarios of a model by repeatedly picking values from the PDFs for all the uncertain variables, and the uncertainty of target (emissions in this case) was determined according to the ranges of calculated scenarios. The PDF was fitted for parameters with adequate measurement data, using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (K-A test) for the goodness-of-fit  $(\\mathrm{p} = 0.05)$ . For parameters with limited measurements, and those that fail to pass the goodness-of-fit test, probability distributions must be assumed. In particular, the uncertainties of activity levels for power sector were taken from Zhao et al. (2011). Small difference was found between the annual coal consumption officially reported for the entire country and that from the sum of provincial consumption of power plants, implying good reliability of statistics for the sector (Zhao et al., 2013). Following the guideline by IPCC (2006), normal distribution was assumed and the coefficients of variation (CV, the standard deviation divided by the mean) was set at  $5\\%$  for activity data of power sector. For other industrial sources, enhanced uncertainty of coal consumption was found through comparisons between the old and updated national energy statistics, and it would lead to an apparent uncertainty of  $38\\%$  in  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions at most (Hong et al., 2017). Accordingly, CV of activity data for industrial sources other than power sector was elevated to  $20\\%$  in this work (i.e., the  $95\\%$  confidence interval (CI) was  $39.2\\%$  around the central estimate). Determination of PDFs for emission factors will be discussed in details in the following section. All of the input", "on uncertainties of Nanjing emissions by sector. As shown in Table 5, emissions and their uncertainties of industrial sector were taken from current work, and emissions from residential and transportation sectors were kept unchanged from Zhao et al. (2015). As little progress was made to improve the uncertainty analysis of city-scale emissions from the latter two sectors, the relative ranges around the central estimates for national inventory were applied (Zhao et al., 2011). For fugitive dust, lognormal distributions with a CV at  $100\\%$  was tentatively assumed to reflect the highly uncertain estimates. With 10,000 Monte-Carlo simulations, the  $95\\%$  CIs of BC to CO and OC to BC emission ratios were calculated at 0.0033–0.0133 and 0.79–2.92, with central estimates at 0.0074 and 1.39, respectively. In contrast, the \"top-down\" constraints from ground observation were 0.0084 and 1.59, respectively, i.e., they were  $14\\%$  larger than the bottom-up ratios, and within the  $95\\%$  CIs. It should be noted that uncertainty also existed in the method of \"top-down\" constraint estimation. For example, the uncertainty of BC to CO emission ratio derived from observation was estimated at  $20\\%$ , as the wet, dry deposition and chemistry of the species could not be fully understood (Wang et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2015). Moreover, the primary OC to BC emission ratio was estimated with a semi-quantitative method, EC-tracer method. The determination of the ratio was arbitrary and unable to obtain single OC to BC ratio that represented a mixture of primary sources varying in time and space. Errors could be caused by the occasional irregular contributions from sources with a primary OC to EC ratio vastly different from the usual mix of sources (Cui et al., 2015). Therefore, the comparison between the bottom-up and top-down estimates needed to be interpreted with caution. Judged by the contribution to the variance of emission ratio, industrial CO and BC emissions were identified to be most significant to the uncertainty of primary BC to CO ratio, with the contribution to variance calculated at  $36\\%$  and  $25\\%$ , respectively. Industrial OC, fugitive OC, and industrial BC emissions were identified to be most significant to uncertainty of primary OC to BC, with the contribution to variance at  $25\\%$ ,  $23\\%$ , and  $20\\%$ , respectively. (5) used to discriminate industrial heat sources from nonindustrial heat sources was difficult to define because the threshold was slightly different under various conditions. When the threshold was too high, some heat sources were missed. When the threshold was too low, some nonindustrial heat sources were extracted. Here, we set the  $\\mathrm{T_0}$  threshold from 0 to  $5\\mathrm{K}$  to identify the behaviors of various thresholds. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the number of derived hot spots decreases as the threshold increases. Meanwhile, the number of incorrect industrial heat sources increases as the threshold decreases (see Fig. 6(b)). To reduce the number of missing hot spots and improve the accuracy of the industrial heat source extraction, we set the threshold to  $1\\mathrm{K}$ .", "With ASTER TIR data, we present a simple thermal anomaly index (TAI) for the rapid extraction of thermal anomaly information according to Wien's law and Planck's law. This index is fast and efficient in detecting hot spots, and can extract several thermal anomalies with low temperatures (i.e., approximately  $400\\mathrm{K}$ ). Then, industrial heat sources are supposed to be derived from those unclassified hot spots. While industrial heat sources derived from time series data (Casadio et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2018, 2016) require a large number of images, we intend to use the VIIRS Day/Night Band (DNB) Nighttime Lights product to simplify this classification process. The reason is that the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program (DMSP) nighttime lights data have already been used for extracting gas flares (Elvidge et al., 2009, 2007) and acted as reference data to confirm the discrimination of flaring sites (Casadio et al., 2012). Although the image for a single date might miss some factories that were not operating when the image was obtained, we aim to operate this TAI to see whether it works.", "Industrial heat sources derived from ASTER data were confirmed with high-resolution images from Google Earth. For more accurate confirmation, the VIIRS Nightfire product V2.1 was used for comparison. The criteria for judging whether a hot spot was an industrial heat source are shown in Fig. 5. Hence, we consider these hot spots, which are confirmed with both confirmed and nonconfirmed VIIRS hot spots (details in Section 2.2) are all high-confidence industrial heat sources. In addition, hot spots evidently located in factories (see Fig. 5(c)) but without any VIIRS hot spots located in the same areas are also considered high-confidence industrial heat sources.", "is common that one VIIRS hot spot corresponds to multiple ASTER hot spots. In this case, we consider that the ASTER hotspots surrounding a VIIRS hotspot are all extracted correctly (see in Fig. 8(b)), although they might be in the same factory. There are a total of 54 hot spots (summed over four dates) via the VIIRS Nightfire product, and 53 hot spots are detected by the TAI. High water vapor and low background temperatures also improve the thermal detection ability of the TAI. content.title: Fig. 8. Hot spots via the VIIRS Nightfire product and hot spots derived via the TAI. (a) An example of the classification of hot spots, (b) the subset of (a), and (c) the results of the classification. Hot spots detected via the VIIRS product indicate that hot spots derived via the TAI surround the hot spots detected via the VIIRS Nightfire product, which belong to the first type of hot spot (hot-spot type details in Section 4.1). Hot spots undetected in the VIIRS indicate that the hot spots derived via the TAI do not surround the hot spots via the VIIRS Nightfire product, which include hot spots of the first and second types.
content.summary: Figure 8 illustrates the classification and distribution of hot spots using VIIRS Nightfire and TAI methods, highlighting detected and undetected hot spots across different dates and regions.
content.insight: The figure demonstrates that TAI detects more hot spots than VIIRS Nightfire, particularly for smaller or less intense industrial heat sources.

content.title: Fig.1 Technical process of industrial heat source object extraction method based on BP neural network

This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. {"id": "img_222", "source_md": "一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_3.jpg", "title": "Fig.3 Spatial distribution of industrial heat source objects", "supplement": "(a) 1667个工业热源对象 (b)各城市工业热源对象数量 图3 工业热源对象空间分布 ", "references": ["(b)各城市工业热源对象数量  
图3 工业热源对象空间分布  
Fig. 3 Spatial distribution of industrial heat source objects", "# An Industrial Heat Source Extraction Method: BP Neural Network Using Temperature Feature Template", "Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. This study reflects the current situation and trend of industrial heat source development in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas, which provides a valuable reference for the formulation and adjustment of relevant policies such as reducing energy consumption and improving secondary industry concentration.", "Key words: industrial heat source; OPTICS algorithm; temperature feature template; neural network; VIIRS Active Fire; Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas", "图1基于BP神经网络的工业热源对象提取方法技术流程

{"id": "img_225", "source_md": "一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\一种工业热源提取方法_利用温度特征模板的BP神经网络\figure_6.jpg", "title": "Fig.6 The number distribution map of industrial thermal anomaly points from 2015 to  2020(10km grid)", "supplement": "(a) 2015年 (b) 2016年 (c) 2017年 (d) 2018年 (e) 2019年 (f) 2020年；工业热异常点 /（数量/个） 一级(1~3) 二级(4~11) 三级(12~41) 四级(42~115) 五级(>115) ； 图6 2015年至2020年工业热异常点数量分布地图 ", "references": ["Abstract: Aiming at the problem of insufficient quantity and spatial refinement in the extraction of industrial heat source from annual scale thermal anomaly data, a neural network industrial heat source extraction method based on temperature feature template is proposed by using VIIRS active fire data. This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Secondly, according to the thermal radiation characteristics of the heat sources, the temperature characteristic template of industrial heat sources and non- industrial heat sources were constructed. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. content.title: Fig.7 FRP value distribution map of industrial heat source from 2015 to 2020 (10 km grid)
content.summary: The figure illustrates the FRP value distribution map for industrial heat sources in the Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei region from 2015 to 2020 at a 10 km grid resolution.
content.insight: The figure highlights the spatial distribution and change trends of industrial heat sources, indicating a reduction in number over the years in the studied region, supporting improved precision through the proposed neural network extraction method.

content.title: Fig.9 Number of thermal anomaly points from January to May
content.summary: The figure shows the number of thermal anomaly points from January to May in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and Wuhan for the years 2019, 2020, and 2021.
content.insight: This figure highlights the impact of COVID-19 on industrial heat sources in Wuhan, showing a significant drop in early 2020 compared to other years, while the impact was minimal in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei.

This study took Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas as the study area, Firstly, according to the spatial aggregation characteristics of industrial heat sources, the heat source objects were divided by the OPTICS algorithm. Finally, the BP neural network was used to extract industrial heat source objects using the temperature feature template and heat source statistical characteristics as parameters. The results show that: (1) the extraction precision of industrial heat source of the neural network algorithm of temperature feature template proposed in this paper reached  $96.31\%$ . Compared with time filtering and logistic regression methods, the extraction precision of industrial heat sources was improved by  $8.45\%$  and  $7.53\%$ , respectively; (2) From 2015 to 2020, the number of industrial heat sources in the six provinces and cities in Beijing-Tianjin- Hebei and its surrounding areas decreased by  $27.46\%$ . The number of industrial heat source objects and heat anomalies in Hebei Province decreased by  $8.06\%$  and  $7.44\%$  annually, respectively, which was the largest decrease compared with other provinces and cities. The", "concentration of industrial heat sources in Shandong and Tianjin increased by  $25.72\%$  and  $86.64\%$ , respectively, indicating that the industrial transformation and upgrade policies in the two places have achieved remarkable results; (3) Tangshan, Handan, Lvliang, and Changzhi accounted for  $31.37\%$  of the total industrial heat sources in the study area, which are the main cities in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas. The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in seven cities such as Linfen and Taiyuan was higher than those in other cities; The degree of industrial heat source accumulation and energy consumption in 11 cities such as Beijing and Zhoukou was lower than those in other cities; (4) From January to May 2020, the number of industrial heat anomalies in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei and its surrounding areas remained unchanged or increased compared with the same period in 2019 and 2021. The COVID-19 had no significant impact on the industrial heat source in the study area. The number of industrial heat anomalies in Wuhan in January and February 2020 decreased by more than  $66.67\%$  compared with that in the same period in 2019 and 2021, the number of industrial heat anomalies from March to May 2020 was lower than that in the same period of 2019. The COVID-19 has had a significant impact on industrial heat sources in Wuhan from January to May 2020. content.title: Fig. 2. The system architecture of this study.
content.summary: The figure illustrates the system architecture used for air quality prediction, covering data collection, preprocessing, model training, and result analysis stages.
content.insight: The architecture supports the study's predictive model combining CNN and LSTM for accurate AQI forecasting across Chinese provincial capitals.

{"id": "img_23", "source_md": "Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md", "save_path": "C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven\figure_2.jpg", "title": "Fig. 2. The system architecture of this study.", "references": ["The Chinese mainland is composed of 33 administrative regions, however, the policies implemented by Hong Kong and Macao special administrative regions differ from the other provinces. Moreover, coal-dominated factories and enterprises are mainly located in the capital city of these provinces, which are typical epitomes of regional environmental pollution. Therefore, a total of 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland are selected. The system architecture of this study is depicted in Fig. 2.", "Considering the data features of high-dimension, multivariable, nonlinearity and temporal-spatial correlation, and with the application of Keras and TensorFlow, a predictive model combining CNN and LSTM is applied and tailored in this study. The architecture and workflows are shown in Fig. 4 and summarized as follows.", "The study obtained the data including a total of 1440 daily air quality and meteorological data (January 22, 2016, to December 31, 2019) from 31 provincial capital cities in the Chinese mainland,  $60\%$  of which are chosen as the training set,  $20\%$  as the validation set and  $20\%$  as the test set. After determining the best basic network architecture for the current prediction tasks, models are set up in units of cities, and the training set is used to train the tailored CNN-LSTM models. The accuracy indicators of the model prediction results of 31 cities, namely RMSE values averaging 22.06, models perform well overall, indicating that the applied CNN-LSTM network has good capabilities for AQI prediction (Jin et al., 2020; Mao et al., 2021).", "Alzubaidi, L., Zhang, J., Humaidi, A.J., Al-Dujaili, A., Duan, Y., Al-Shamma, O., Santamaría, J., Fadhel, M.A., Al-Amidie, M., Farhan, L., 2021. metadata.full_json: {"id": "img_179", "source_md": "Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its.md", "save_path": "C:\\liuxingyu\\multisubspace-data\\predataprocess\\paper_md\\Progress of Air Pollution Control in China and Its\\figure_5.jpg", "title": "Fig. 5. Trend of annual average  PM_{2.5}  concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018. Observed  PM_{2.5}  concentrations were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center.", "references": ["Guided by the Action Plan, many other cities in China are following Beijing to reduce ambient  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations (Fig. 5). The strategic promotion of ecological civilization has spurred stronger willingness, clearer targets, more supportive legislation and incentives, and more stringent implementation of control actions from central to local governments. All these factors work together with public engagement to achieve progress in controlling  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution and improving public health and welfare. During the Action Plan 2013-2017,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations deceased by  $23\\%$  on average for all the municipal cities in China [46] (Fig. 5). Reductions for the three key regions were even more significant:  $40\\%$  for BTH,  $34\\%$  for YRD, and  $28\\%$  for PRD. The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration in the PRD region was  $34~\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\cdot \\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , which adhered to NAAQS for  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . This progress has motivated some local governments to actively target more stringent limits on  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$", "(b)  \nFig. 6. (a) Trends in observed annual  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations for BTH and 74 key cities between 2013 and 2017; (b) trends expressed as 90th percentiles in maximum daily average  $8\\mathrm{hO}_3$  concentration. Shaded areas represent the 25th-75th percentile range. Data are available in Table S1 in Appendix A. Details on monthly  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration for 74 key cities are summarized in Tables S2-S6.  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentrations in the BTH region and 74 cities were obtained from the China National Environmental Monitoring Center."], "functional_roles": {"main_role": "Results & Findings", "sub_roles": ["Present Core Findings", "Present Quantitative Results"]}, "visual_type": {"main_type": "Quantitative Charts", "sub_types": ["Heatmap"]}, "key_entities": ["PM2.5 concentrations", "China National Environmental Monitoring Center", "Beijing", "ecological civilization", "Action Plan"], "concise_summary": "Heatmap showing annual average PM2.5 concentrations for the top 20 most populated cities in China from 2013 to 2018, highlighting trends in air quality improvements.", "inferred_insight": "The figure illustrates significant improvements in PM2.5 concentrations due to strategic ecological campaigns and government interventions in China's major cities."}

Seasonal spatial distribution of the LAI in the model domain.", "supplement": "(a).spring;(b).summer;(c).autumn;(d).winter", "references": ["MEGAN v2.1 (Guenther et al. 2012) was utilized to estimate BVOC emissions in China with a  $27\mathrm{-km}$  horizontal grid spacing domain (shown in Fig. 1). The inputs for MEGAN include the LAI, PFTs, emission factors, and meteorological data (e.g., solar radiation, temperature, relative humidity and soil moisture). The monthly average LAI was obtained from the 8-day MODIS LAI product (MOD15A2, 2017) with the same horizontal resolution as the study domain, as shown in Fig. 2. The PFT map was obtained from the MODIS MCD12Q1 product and regridded into the WRF domain to compute the fraction of each PFT in each grid (Fig. 3). The default MEGAN emission factors  $(\mathrm{mg~m}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1})$  with a resolution of  $30\mathrm{s}$ $(< 1\mathrm{km})$ , which reflect the BVOC emission rate under standard canopy conditions, were leveraged (http://lar.wsu.edu/megan/guides.html). Meteorological conditions were simulated by the WRF model. A one-year MEGAN simulation was performed for 2017 in China.", "Fig. S1 and S2 show the seasonal spatial distribution of temperature at  $2\mathrm{m}$  (T2) and the daily downward shortwave radiation (DSW) simulated by the WRF model. And these meteorological factors were evaluated using in situ measurement data from 824 and 84 national meteorological sites, respectively. The in situ measurement data are from the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://data.cma.cn/). Table 2 presents the verification statistics for the average daily T2 and DSW among all sites. As shown in Table 2, the mean error (ME), mean bias (MB), correlation coefficient (r) and root mean square error (RMSE) of station-averaged hourly T2 series are 2.47, -1.26, 0.95 and  $2.59^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively. The simulation yields cooling biases of  $-0.98$ ,  $-0.90$ ,  $-1.70$  and  $-1.18^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. The ME, MB, r and RMSE values for the annual DSW series are 36.00,  $-29.88$ , 0.80 and  $81.33\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$ , and the DSW simulation yields underestimations of 32.12, 43.61, 24.25 and  $19.38\mathrm{Wm}^{-2}$  in spring, summer, fall and winter, respectively. It is because that the WRF model overestimate the cloud coverage which lead to the slightly underestimation on T2 and DSW (Wang et al. 2010; Wen et al. 2014). Compared with other studies on yearly long WRF simulation in China (Wang et al., 2014; Ying et al. 2014; Zhang et al., 2012), these biases are", "Fig. 3. Seasonal distributions of the proportions of PFTs in the model domain.", "Fig. 7a-c shows the simulated and observed daily 1-h maximum  $\mathrm{O_3}$ $(\mathrm{DM1O_3})$  concentration over China in July 2017. In general, the model successfully captured the spatial pattern over the simulated domain with highest  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  areas centralized in the east, especially in the BTH, PRD, YRD and SCB regions due to the relatively large  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and VOC emission and favorable meteorological conditions for ozone formation in these areas (e.g., little rain and strong solar radiation). And model", "It should be noted that the simulated SOA concentrations in this study have not been compared with direct measurements of organic particulate matter and SOA because of the data limitations. Extensive measurements of organic components have been conducted in megacities and major clusters in China by aerosol mass spectrometers (AMS), which provides details about various organic aerosols (Hu et al. 2016; Yang et al., 2016). And these data can be used as model validation for future local scale SOA modeling studies which can promote the understanding of SOA formation. Besides, a great deal of additional research is needed to further address the remaining questions. For example, to better quantify the separate and synergistic effects of anthropogenic and VOC emissions based on sensitivity testing, it is necessary to investigate the spatial distribution of ozone changes by comparing NOx and VOC emissions. Both chamber studies and field measurements have shown that HOMs (highly oxygenated molecules) can substantially increase the SOA mass because of their low volatility (Ehn et al. 2014). This mechanism has already been incorporated into box models (Öström et al. 2017) with detailed chemistry, and it would be interesting to observe the impact of this mechanism on SOA formation from BVOCs at the regional scale.", "Nat.
